author: JB
text: The one idea I'd flag as the live wire: **delivery ≠ triggering**. push-updates already answers "how a tier-1 item reaches an idle session" (Channels). What's still open — and where the actual differentiation is — is **what earns escalation**. That's a slice you could ship before idle-push is unblocked.

author: JB
text: One thing the workflow deliberately does **not** do: commit. That stays with me after I've reviewed the verdict — so you get a clean single "reset to skeleton" commit rather than five agents' worth of noise.

# clair

clair gives you and your agents repo-level **ambient awareness** of everyone else's
activity, via **progressive disclosure**: *a clair discloses a live event the way a
skill discloses pre-defined context.* The full vision and its single source of truth
live in [docs/product.md](docs/product.md).

author: JB
text: This is a deep, multi-source research task — using the deep-research harness. The request is broad but our context is rich, so I'll scope it tightly to clair's actual angles (proactive collaboration-awareness, git-as-transport, ambient/progressive-disclosure UX, and the multi-agent AI coordination space) rather than ask you to narrow it — "tools or thinking" tells me you want both the competitive landscape and the conceptual lineage.

author: JB
text:

<output-file>C:\Users\bogwe\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\F--dev-clair\6d0b7e22-7f6c-41cb-8fe1-1d1a12dc17f6\tasks\wwx1s11lp.output</output-file>

<summary>Dynamic workflow "Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report." completed</summary>
<result>{"question":"Research prior art and current thinking relevant to \"clair\" — a tool that gives software developers and their AI coding agents AMBIENT AWARENESS of everyone else's activity across a git repository, using PROGRESSIVE DISCLOSURE (a cheap always-on headline that escalates to detail only when relevant) and using GIT ITSELF as the transport (ephemeral orphan/shadow refs, no server). Core premise: in a near future where most developers and AI agents work solo on their own branches/worktrees, the valuable thing is loose, repo-level awareness of what others are doing — surfaced as a \"proximity radar\" in the editor statusline that stays quiet until a peer's work (spatially: same files/hunks; or semantically: a relevant decision/finding) closes in on yours.\n\nCover these dimensions, with concrete named tools/papers, dates, and how each relates (validates, overlaps, or differs):\n\n1. COLLABORATION-AWARENESS & PROACTIVE CONFLICT DETECTION research (CSCW / software engineering): tools and papers on workspace awareness and predicting/surfacing merge conflicts early — e.g. Palantír, Crystal, Cassandra, WeCode, Syde, FASTDash, CollabVS, and similar. What did they surface, how, and why did most not become mainstream? This is the closest academic lineage to clair's proximity feature.\n\n2. AMBIENT AWARENESS / SOCIAL TRANSLUCENCE theory: Erickson & Kellogg's \"social translucence,\" ambient/peripheral displays, awareness vs. interruption/notification cost. Progressive disclosure as a UX principle (Nielsen) and its application to notifications and developer tools.\n\n3. GIT-AS-A-DATABASE / GIT-AS-TRANSPORT prior art: tools that store metadata or messages on git refs/notes/orphan branches rather than a server — e.g. git-notes, git-bug, git-appraise, git-town, GitTorrent, Radicle, Dolt, Fossil. Does anyone use ephemeral/orphan refs as an ad-hoc message bus? Validates or challenges clair's \"no server, git is the pipe\" moat.\n\n4. MULTI-AGENT AI CODING COORDINATION (2024–2026, most important and most current): how do parallel AI coding agents share state, memory, or awareness of each other? Look at Claude Code (subagents, worktrees, hooks, statusline, MCP), Cursor, Devin/Cognition, OpenAI Codex/Swarm, agent orchestration frameworks, \"agent inboxes,\" shared-context/shared-memory systems, and any product explicitly doing cross-agent or cross-branch presence/awareness. Is anyone building \"ambient awareness for AI agents working in one repo\"? Who is closest to clair, and how do they differ?\n\n5. LIVE COLLABORATION / PAIRING tools clair deliberately is NOT (for positioning): VS Code Live Share, JetBrains Code With Me, Tuple, CRDT-based shared editing. Note why clair shares AI context/awareness rather than live bytes.\n\n6. DEVELOPER PRESENCE / STATUS tools: anything showing \"who is working where\" in a codebase (e.g. GitHub presence, GitKraken, Waydev, git-pulse-style dashboards, editor presence plugins).\n\nDeliver: a synthesized, cited report that maps the landscape, identifies clair's genuinely novel ground vs. existing work, names the strongest precedents and the strongest current competitors (especially in the AI-agent space), and flags any prior failures (e.g. conflict-awareness tools that didn't stick) whose lessons clair should heed.","summary":"Synthesis step was skipped or failed — returning 14 verified claims unmerged.","findings":[],"confirmed":[{"claim":"Palantír is a workspace awareness tool that breaks 'bad isolation' in configuration management by continuously sharing information about which developers are changing which artifacts in parallel, before any merge/integration occurs — directly the academic ancestor of clair's spatial proximity awareness.","source":"https://web.engr.oregonstate.edu/~sarmaa/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/01201222.pdf","quote":"we have built Palantír, a novel configuration management workspace awareness tool that deliberately breaks bad isolation while retaining good isolation... Palantír informs a developer of which other developers change which other artifacts, calculates a simple measure of severity of those changes, and graphically displays the information in a configurable and generally non-obtrusive manner.","vote":"3-0"},{"claim":"Palantír's key mechanism is inverting information flow from pull to push: instead of learning about others' work only when you perform a CM operation (check-in/check-out/query), it continuously pushes change events from every workspace — the conceptual precursor to clair's always-on ambient transport.","source":"https://web.engr.oregonstate.edu/~sarmaa/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/01201222.pdf","quote":"A crucial aspect of Palantír is that it inverts information flow from pull to push. Instead of informing developers of other efforts only when they themselves perform some configuration management operation (e.g., check in or check out), Palantír increases awareness by continuously sharing information regarding operations performed by all developers.","vote":"3-0"},{"claim":"Palantír computes a quantitative 'severity' metric — e.g. lines added/removed/changed divided by total lines in an artifact — to escalate attention from a mere change indicator to a signal of likely future conflict; this is an early form of progressive disclosure (cheap signal escalating to detail).","source":"https://web.engr.oregonstate.edu/~sarmaa/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/01201222.pdf","quote":"A trivial severity measure is binary and simply indicates whether or not any kind of change has occurred. A slightly more complicated measure of severity can be calculated by dividing the number of lines that has been added, removed, and changed by the total number of lines in an artifact... Small changes typically will be easy to reconcile. Large changes may signify a regression towards potentially difficult conflicts in an eventual integration.","vote":"3-0"},{"claim":"Crystal detects conflicts by speculative analysis — it actually performs the version-control merge, build, and test on background clones rather than predicting/estimating conflicts heuristically. This directly validates clair's premise of using git operations themselves (and computing, not guessing) but differs in that Crystal aims to compute exact pending merge conflicts rather than provide loose ambient proximity awareness.","source":"https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~rtholmes/papers/fse_2011_brun.pdf","quote":"Speculative analysis [4] does not guess at possible conflicts. Instead, it speculatively performs the work and executes the VCS operations in the background on clones of the program: it actually merges George's and Ringo's committed code, builds it, and runs its tests. This allows speculative analysis to deliver precise information about conflicts","vote":"3-0"},{"claim":"Crystal delivers its awareness through progressive disclosure: a compact always-visible icon summary plus a single system-tray icon showing the most-severe state, with full detail hidden until the developer mouses over an item. This is essentially clair's cheap-headline-escalating-to-detail UX, demonstrated in a 2011 conflict-awareness tool.","source":"https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~rtholmes/papers/fse_2011_brun.pdf","quote":"Full, detailed information about each relationship, action, and guidance is available but hidden until a developer shows specific interest in it. Crystal uses (1) icons exploiting color and shape in stable locations in the main window ... (2) a taskbar icon in the system tray to report the most severe state for all tracked repositories, and (3) mouse-over tooltips that provide, on demand, all the information discussed","vote":"2-0"},{"claim":"Crystal positions itself against pure awareness tools (Palantir, FASTDash, Syde, CollabVS) by arguing those tools generate false-positive warnings from exploratory edits, and that Crystal avoids this by only reporting conflicts after code is committed to a repository. This is the documented failure-mode lesson clair sho

text: Note on the "refuted" claims: they weren't genuinely refuted — they failed verification as **rate-limit abstentions** (`0-0, 3 abstain`), and they're textbook facts (Gutwin & Greenberg workspace awareness, Nielsen progressive disclosure, social translucence). I'll treat those as solid. The real gap is that verification ran out of budget on the **academic angle** and never reached the git-transport and AI-agent claims — so those are *extracted but unverified*. Let me pull them from the raw output before I write.

author: JB
text: Matching = match(my_work, clair.about) → score, computed as weighted overlap: path prefix/glob ∩, symbol exact ∩, tag exact ∩. Deterministic, local, cheap enough for the 1-second statusline poll. Why facets over embeddings: the landscape research shows the spatial signal (same file/hunk) is the proven, cheap win; a free-text/embedding step is heavier, can't reliably run in <1s locally, and is fuzzier to query. Semantic matching is layered on later behind the same match() interface — facets first, embeddings as a pluggable upgrade, never a blocker. :: wtf is that all about lol, you need to explain all that, glob? those strange alien symbols? I do not understand how to even begin reading it

author: JB
text: This is substantial and load-bearing enough to be its own spec — it's the materialized-view layer sitting between the data model and the transport. author: JB
text: yes we should document it now. We need to think abou token efficiency, latency as well. author: JB
text: Want me to commit these two, or keep going and batch the commit?
